speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Aaron Elster. Were there some other things that took place before the liberation? Perhaps while you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, certain things that I recall. You sit in the attic, and you really have-- I can't read. I have nothing to read. I have nothing to do. So you're trying to figure out how to spend some time. And sometimes I would separate the thin sheets to be able to look down into the yard. And one of the tenants had a little girl. And I remember her. She always used to be in the backyard. And she would stand there and eat out of a dish of some kind of food. And I would be so envious of her and so-- I would actually curse her for having what she had and me being in my situation. And I guess sometimes I used to go stir crazy in the attic. I used to run around. It was a row attic, and I used to run around. And she would hear me. And the parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: She?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl insisted that there was somebody up there. And the parents told her no, it's rats or mice up there. So one day she insisted that she wants to go and look. And the parents brought her up to convince her that there was nobody up there. And I hid in the extreme corner-- it was totally dark-- hoping that they wouldn't see me. But one of the tenants saw me. And he turned around and told his daughter, see, there's nobody here. And they left. And I thought that perhaps that was going to be the end, that they were going to turn us in. He was a schoolteacher, I later found out. And nobody said a word. There were things that happened during that time that were-- they're hard for me to even believe what transpired. Constantly being cold. In the wintertime, there was no heat in there. And I had no other clothes except for that blanket and that straw that I was laying on. And being hungry. And the hunger was so deep, that I would have done anything for any kind of food. In fact it's an irony now. God plays tricks with you. When you can't afford to eat or you don't have any to eat, your system will absorb nails. Now that I can eat anything that I want, I got heart trouble and I've got to eat chicken salad. So I think God plays games with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I always found that I couldn't understand how can you have enough food? And it took years for me to finally get to a point where I wasn't thinking about that. But I didn't think I was going to survive that. We really didn't, because of the threat from the people, and because it was so long, so long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the news from the front was always bad. And the Germans were killing the Poles, they were killing-- for the slightest infraction they were killing people. But anyway, we made it through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of leaving the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Trying to go anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? Where? Where? Sure, I would have loved to, but I was afraid. This was my safety. The attic was my safety. This was the safest place I could possibly be. If I would go outside, I couldn't possibly survive. I looked different. I was different. And when you live in a small community, you could recognize an outsider, and especially the Poles. They had a nose to recognize Jews, I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you look Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I did or didn't. I was dark hair. My features were not that abnormal where I-- I don't think so, but I was always afraid that if somebody wants to know if you're Jewish, it's very easy. Drop your drawers and that's it. So anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about your family while you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And trying to realize-- I'm trying to think who did I feel worse for? Did I feel bad for them because they were dead, or did I feel bad for myself because I was orphaned? And I've never really been able to resolve that, because there's-- there are feelings there of the pain and the suffering that they went through in dying, and also of me being alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've been alone since I was what? Eight, nine years old. And at my age now, it seems trivial, but all my years, I've never had anybody that said to me I love you or that I care for you. Or-- I never thought about it, because I didn't-- I thought it was-- that was my lot in life. And I accepted that. But now, in retrospect, I think about a lot of things. What it's like to grow up, to be knocked around from one orphanage to another orphanage, to have to eat in different families' homes as a beggar, because there was nobody there to take care of you? And the people didn't have enough food to take care of themselves. I was taking food away from them. And so they would send me from one house to another house. And that created a tremendous resentment for those people, not just being grateful, just being resentful that I had to go through this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether I'm making myself clear but-- so anyway, that's what started happening after the war. I thought, the war is over, I'm liberated, things would be fine. But we come back, there's only 23 to 27 people that survived from the whole town of 6,000 people. I have no family. I have no house to go to. It's just us. And this is when it started with being bounced around from one home to another home, being fed in one place and being fed another place. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you wonder what was it all about. If you're going to survive, there should be an end. There should be a conclusion to the suffering. There should be better. OK. So, but I still kept hoping. But something good did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you hoping? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just the will. I had tremendous will. Tremendous will power to-- to live-- to for-- I've always lived for tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never got into her head. But apparently she had-- she has her own. We tell two different stories. We lived together. We tell two different stories, because our minds are different. Our-- our-- our-- our life was somewhat different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I never thought about getting into her head, how she felt. In fact, I never thought about getting into my own head. This is just of a recent nature that you started thinking about psychology and all that type of stuff. This was the way it was, and you accepted it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle showed up. I had an uncle-- his name is Sam. He's alive, living in New York-- that escaped to Russia in 1939. Him and a couple of his brothers. Well, they made it through to the Russian zone. Then they got captured by the Germans. They were in the ghetto. Then he wound up in the partisans. The Russian partisans. These stories that he has are fantastic. He survived with the partisans, and then he got into the Polish army when the Polish army was created, when the Germans were being chased out. So he came back as a Polish sergeant. And to me he was god. And he came, and he found us, found my sister and I, and started taking care of us. But we had no future in Poland, because some of the people that would come back to regain their properties, if they would go out of the town, going to the farms, the Poles would kill them. So there was no end to that. And we decided, all of us, that we were going to leave Sokolow, go to a bigger city where there was a greater population, and from there, try to emigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took us to Lodz. It was a big city. And first time I really saw lights and inside plumbing. Which reminds me, when we were at home, before the war, even during the war-- you're talking about my sister-- when she would have to go at night, she would have to pay me for me to go outside with her to talk to her while she was sitting in the outhouse. So I would converse with her while she was sitting in the outhouse. And then it was so dark, I'd get scared. I'd run in the house and leave her there. But that was our lifestyle. Anyway, my uncle-- Could you spell Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz? L-O-D-Z. We were there for a little while, and then from there I was sent to an orphanage in the Carpathian Mountains, which was wonderful for me. I was there with children. There was food, lots of food. Fresh air. I was able to play with kids. I had a really good time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was in Lodz with my uncle. And then my uncle came to get me.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, probably two months. And he came to get me. He says, we're leaving Poland. We're going to go to Germany. So-- oh. By the way, he escaped from Poland, got rid of his army uniform, and we all wanted to get the hell out. So he made a deal with some Russian soldier to take us to the border, to the Polish Czech border. And we smuggled across into the Czech border. And we went to Prague. In Prague, we got there at night. And Prague was all lit up. And I thought was the most beautiful city I've ever seen. And we went into a DP camp. A displaced persons camp. And we stayed there just shortly. And from there, my uncle made a deal with somebody to get us to the German border. And we got to the German border. We got-- got on a train-- we didn't have any passports-- hoping that we can smuggle across to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he want to take you to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Americans were there. There was the American zone. That was the most important place to go to. We were stopped by the border. The American MPs threw us off the train and sent us back to Czechoslovakia. Well, we tried it again. And we got to the border again. On the train, as the border guards were coming in one side, we jumped off the train on the other side and ran into the woods. There was a whole bunch of us. We made it through. We made it off. We made it over to Germany. And the first town I think we hit was a town called Hofen in Germany. And from there we went to a displaced persons camp right outside Nuremberg. And there we stayed. The UNRRA, U-N-N-R-A, would provide food for us. And we had clothes. And I became bar mitzvah in Germany in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle decided that I should become bar mitzvah. I don't even know how he decided on what date, because I didn't know when I was born. So apparently they just, hey, I must be 13, so let's make him bar mitzvah. So I became bar mitzvah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it something you wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall whether I wanted it or not. That was never too-- I learned how to do with the tallis and the [NON-ENGLISH] and all that. It didn't last too long, but I did do it initially. I was fairly fluent in Hebrew in that time, because you started Hebrew at four or five years of age. So we lived in that camp. And that camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the first camp. But it was right outside Nuremberg. And a bunch of partisans from Poland wound up in that camp, and they were a very rowdy group. They would do all kinds of crazy things. They would steal. They would do all kinds of crazy things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally they-- they decided to close that camp. And we went to another DP camp called Furth outside-- right outside Nuremberg. And that was also 1945. Did you get to know any of these partisans who were in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. But I wouldn't remember the names. Sure. I used to hang around with all adults. There was no kids to hang around with. I was 25 years old when I was 9. I mean, here. There was no childhood. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we lived in Furth for a while. And I used to go to Nuremberg all the time, because the trials were going on at that time. All the-- all the-- all the trials, the Nuremberg Trials were going on. I used to go there to try and-- And who were they trying?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were trying all the-- Goebbels, all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forget their names by now. All the Nazis. There was a thing for me to do. It was a place to go. And I remember going there all the time. Anyway, we stayed in Furth for about probably three, four months. That was in '46 already. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of kid were you in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty wild. I was on my own. I did what I pretty well pleased. Nobody told me what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody to tell me what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to be in the black market. I used to hang around with American GIs. I used to get candy from them and cigarettes from them. And then I'd bring it back and sell it. There was no school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I did what was necessary. I hate to-- you know, I did what was necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the things. If I had to steal something, I would steal something. It was a means of survival. Looking back, now, at it, I'm probably not too proud of the things, but that's-- that was my lot in life. I was very rebellious. There was no control on me. We went to another camp called Munich. Not Munich. Neu Freimann, outside Munich, in Germany. That was in 1946. And I started school, started traditional public school, and also Hebrew school, because we were preparing to go to Israel. And I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you doing this because you wanted to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go anyplace. I wanted to go America. I wanted to go to Israel. I mean, I would go any place. And I stayed with my uncle and my sister in a one room apartment in that camp. But then my uncle found a girlfriend. And he was very active. He was a soccer player. So he moved out of there. And then my sister moved out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wound up staying with a couple. They moved a couple in there. A young couple. And it was very-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was not a very pleasant experience for me, for me to lay here and a couple making love in a bed over there. They thought I was too young to understand what was going on. But I was already an old man at 13 years of age, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we stayed there. And I used to eat in the public kitchen. And I hated the food. So they had peanut butter sandwiches. And I loved peanut butter. To this date I love peanut butter and jelly. And I ate peanut butter for probably months at a time. OK. Went to school, traveled by train from-- from-- from one place to another to sell candy bars and cigarettes in a rucksack, and never bought a ticket on a train. The conductor would come. I would go on the back. He would go on the back. I would go on the front. It was just one of those things. Then we went to kibbutz in Germany to prepare to go to Israel. And my uncle was living already with that girl's family and so on. And my sister and I went to that kibbutz in Germany. Someplace near Bayreuth was a farm to get prepared to go to Israel. And I had a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it from my uncle. And they found out I had it, and they wanted it from me. And I didn't want to give it up. So they beat me up pretty good. I still didn't give them the gun. I would go in the back in the fields and just lay around there all day. Just didn't want to have anything to do with anybody there. And my sister would work in the kitchen. And that lasted for-- for-- for maybe three, four, months or something, because we were in Germany for close to two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in what? 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946 Going towards 40-- going towards '47. Then we came back to Neu Freimann, because we found that we had relatives in the United States. And frankly, I wanted to go the United States instead of Israel, because I always heard it was so hot there. I always say, everybody said it was so hot there. So I wanted to go to the United States, because after all, I found relatives. Relative turned out to be that was my grandfather's brother, like a second, third uncle, or something like that. And we were sponsored to go to the United States. And we left Germany on June 7, 1947 on the came to New York, went into an orphanage. They gave me $2. First thing I did, went downstairs. There was a candy store. Bought $2 worth of candy and ice cream bars and ate all my old belongings up. That was the greatest time I had. And then I used to take the buses in New York. And if it wasn't crowded, I would lay down on the seats to look up at the buildings and the lights. It was really marvelous. I mean, it was such a joy to-- everything was such a joy. Food was a joy. Seeing things was a joy. Experiencing things was a joy. It was really-- it was good times in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in that orphanage, and then we were supposed to go live with my aunt and uncle in Chicago. So we went there. We stayed there for a while with them. It didn't really work out, and my sister moved out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was sponsored by an organization called the Jewish Children's Bureau, the JCB. They took me under their care, and they put me into a foster home. And I stayed in a few foster homes. And one of my first social workers was a lady by the name of Sutker, S-U-T-K-E-R. In fact, her husband is Calvin Sutker. He was a politician in the area. Had a tremendous impact on my life, this woman, because she was so kind and gentle and caring. And I guess I responded to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran into her not too long ago. And I went up to her. She was sitting there having lunch with her daughter and her husband. I told her what a great impact she made on my life. It was a good experience. I have a very good feeling for her in my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, when I came to United States, I came to Chicago. They put me into fourth grade grammar school, because I didn't speak English. I knew how to swear in English, because I learned that from the GIs in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. Can you imagine me sitting in the fourth grade? The school teacher was only 23 or 24 years of age. I sat there for maybe three, four weeks, then they pushed me into sixth grade, because I had to learn the language. And I started hanging around the park. And I learned the language fairly well, in fact. I used to speak Polish, Jewish. When I was in Germany, I learned how to speak German. When I was living with the Russians in Poland, I understood Russian, and so on. I still remember a lot of Russian songs, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I was in sixth grade for a while. And then they pushed me into seven grade for a month or so. And I finished the year in eighth grade and graduated. So it took me a year to go through grammar school. And then I went to high school. And it was not a very good experience, high school for me, because I didn't start high school till I was 16 years of age. What high school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started at Farragut High School on the west side of Chicago. And then I moved to Albany Park, and I went Von Steuben High School, which at that time was more of an upper class area, fairly affluent people. And I used to come with my salami sandwiches on Silvercup bread and put it in my locker. And nobody would come near me because I would stink from salami so bad. Anyway, I tried to fit in. I played soccer, so I got on the team. I tried to become Americanized. I started wearing the American's type clothes and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you live with at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. And Mrs. Brooks. They had three, four foster children at a time. She was a great lady. Had a mouth on her. Scream, yell. Just to avoid her screaming and yelling, I would volunteer to do the dishes. I would do anything just to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Experiences at the Brooks' house when I was living there. Remember I told you about not being able to know what it's like to be full? I would steal food in that house. There was no reason for it. She finally came up to me and says, what the hell are you doing? I mean, you can eat whatever you want. Why are you stealing food?

speaker: interviewee
text: At 16, 17 years old I used to have nightmares. Nobody would come and wake me up. They would use a broom handle to come near me. I would automatically jump out of bed. I used to also urinate in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not being aware--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your nightmares like, do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being chased by Germans. Being almost killed. Being almost killed. Being almost done this. All these nightmares for many, many years. I think I probably started having nightmares after I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you stopped having nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stopped having nightmares, probably after I got married. So I went to high school. I graduated from high school. And I wanted to go to college. I couldn't. I went to Wright Junior College for a short period of time. And then I volunteered to go into the Army, because the draft at that time and the war was going on in Korea. And I thought maybe they were going to send me to Germany, because I knew the language. My luck, they sent me to Korea. So I spent two years in Korea. And I worked for a company called O'Connor and Goldberg when I was going to high school. And I came out of--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Korea, what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a supply sergeant. I didn't know what to do with myself. I had no particular skills or anything, but I went to typing school, learned how to type, and suddenly I was working the supply room. And suddenly the supply sergeant gets transferred out. I'm promoted to supply sergeant. It was a fairly good experience, although initially I didn't think so. Basic training was murder. And Korea was very poor at that time. Extremely poor. And it rained for four or five weeks at a time. But it was a good experience. The Army was a good experience for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it a good experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: It strengthened me physically. It also-- I don't know. I just felt good when I-- when I got out. It also gave me a certain amount of power. I felt empowered. Yeah. I did. I used to have dreams of joining the Air Force and stealing an atomic bomb and bomb Germany out of existence, for many years. Never got the chance to do that. But I would've gladly done that. That's how I felt about it. I came out of the-- when I was still going to high school, I met my wife Jackie. We had a long distance engagement. She lived on the south side. I lived on the west side. And I got married to Jackie, 1954, during my furlough when I was in the Army. And as soon as I got married, we were separated for 18 months. When I came back, she had an apartment all fixed up for us and did a great job. And I didn't know what to do with my life. I was working in a shoe store, and I didn't think that this was the thing that I wanted to do the rest of my life. And the only background I had was I felt I-- I-- I-- I could sell. I could convince people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got into the insurance business, scared out of my wits. Scared of failure. Always scared of failure. Never having enough of confidence in myself that I would be able to support a family, to buy the necessities to provide my children with whatever was necessary. Extremely preoccupied with that, that I would never be able to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in spite of myself, I succeeded beyond my expectations. I became a district manager with the company, and I went to sales. I was a leading salesman for the territory, which is like a 16 state area. They voted me into the Hall of Fame with Metropolitan. I recently retired with an extremely generous pension. I have a loving family. I have two sons. One is married, has two, my two granddaughters. I have two granddaughters. I have another son out in California that's also living with a lady. I love them both. I think they love me, and I feel fairly satisfied with my life at this point. I really never thought that I would be in this position. So it did work out. But I'm still somewhat of a masochist, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't enjoy what I have, not to the extent that I probably should. I still don't feel that I'm entitled. I feel that it's all going to disappear. Somebody's going to take it away from me. That I'm really not entitled to this. The suffering I was entitled to. The hunger I was entitled to. The fears I was entitled to. But the good things, for some odd reason I'm not convinced. And I try to tell myself all the time now, hey, you're entitled. It's working. I've-- it's working. It's working a lot better.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I have a problem with my religion. I'm not comfortable in the synagogue, because I forgot how to pray in Hebrew. Now I have to look on the other side of the page to pray. I had a problem with God. Why would God allow this to happen? And if he did allow it to happen as a punishment to his people, why the children? I have trouble with that. Have you come to any kind of resolution about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I'll ever totally resolve it. I'm becoming more at peace with myself than I used to be, but it never goes away. And I've talked to different people, questioning. I don't like dogma. I really despise dogma. I despise right wings, whether it's my religion or anybody else's religion. I strongly believe that in the name of God and the name of religion, the greatest atrocities are being committed. And I have a problem with that. I made some mistakes with my children, because when they were growing up I didn't want to put any baggage on their shoulders with my background. So I overcompensated. I wanted them to be their own people. In fact, I didn't-- my children didn't have bar mitzvahs. In my own mind, I felt that they should be able to make their own decisions. Which I realize now that was wrong, because I gave them too much freedom at that time. But what comes around what-- goes around comes around. My son is now going to Hebrew school. He's 38 years old. He wants to become bar mitzvah, and I'm the proudest person I could possibly be. In fact, I go to Hebrew school with him. I figure if it's good enough for him, it's good enough for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in their own way, they-- they-- they are doing certain things that I would've wanted them to do. But I wasn't sure how to handle it. I didn't know how to be a parent.
